context:
Section 7 (Concluding comments), page 29 (PDF pagination shown in provided text)

Competitiveness is much harder to adapt. Its definition generalises when we have only upper bounds, but we lose the critical fixed point argument in Lemma 1 for the existence of a randomised competitive congestion profile.

With lower bounds it is not clear what the definition of competitiveness should be: how to account for the situation where a lower bound \(\underline{\sigma}_a\) forces the agents to populate a post \(a\) that they unanimously loath?
title: Define competitive fair random assignments with lower congestion bounds on posts
background_latex:
There is a finite set of agents \(N\) with \(|N|=n\), and a finite set of posts \(A\) with \(|A|=m\). An assignment is a family \(P=(S_a)_{a\in A}\) of pairwise disjoint subsets of \(N\) whose union is \(N\); agent \(i\in S_a\) is assigned to post \(a\). Under anonymous (unweighted) congestion, the congestion (occupancy) of post \(a\) in assignment \(P\) is \(s_a=|S_a|\), and the congestion profile is \(s=(s_a)_{a\in A}\).

Each agent \(i\) has preferences over allocations \((a,s)\) consisting of a post \(a\in A\) and an integer congestion level \(s\in\{1,2,\dots,n\}\). Preferences are strictly decreasing in the congestion coordinate: for each fixed \(a\), \((a,s)\) is strictly preferred to \((a,s+1)\).

A randomized assignment induces an expected congestion profile \(\sigma=(\sigma_a)_{a\in A}\) with \(\sum_{a\in A}\sigma_a=n\) and \(\sigma_a\ge 0\). Given cardinal utilities \(u_i(a,s)\) (strictly decreasing in \(s\) for fixed \(a\)), one can extend to non-integer congestion via linear interpolation in \(s\), yielding a continuous function \(u_i(a,\sigma_a)\) for \(\sigma_a\in[1,n]\).

In the baseline model without hard bounds, a fractional expected congestion profile \(\sigma\) is called competitive if it can be generated by assigning each agent with a probability distribution supported on their competitive-demand posts, where the competitive demand of agent \(i\) at profile \(\sigma\) is
\[
D(u_i;\sigma)=\arg\max_{x\in A} u_i\bigl(x,\,\sigma_x\vee 1\;\bigr),
\]
with \(\vee\) denoting \(\max\). The paper proves existence and (qualified) uniqueness of such a competitive fractional congestion profile in the anonymous-congestion setting.

Now consider adding post-specific lower and upper bounds on congestion. Let \((\ell_a)_{a\in A}\) and \((u_a)_{a\in A}\) be integers with \(0\le \ell_a\le u_a\le n\). A deterministic assignment \(P\) is feasible with bounds if \(\ell_a\le s_a\le u_a\) for all \(a\in A\). A randomized assignment is feasible with bounds if its realized deterministic assignments satisfy these bounds (or, alternatively, if one imposes constraints on expected congestion; the paper highlights difficulty particularly due to lower bounds).
problem_definition_latex: Formulate an appropriate notion of competitiveness (an envy-freeness/market-clearing-style ex post fairness concept) for congested assignment problems with anonymous congestion when each post \(a\in A\) is subject to a lower bound \(\ell_a\) (and possibly also an upper bound \(u_a\)) on its congestion. In particular, specify how competitiveness should treat posts forced to have occupancy at least \(\ell_a\) even when all agents strictly dislike being assigned there at those congestion levels, and provide a definition that can serve as a basis for selecting a fair randomized assignment in such bounded-congestion settings.
latex:
There is a finite set of agents \(N\) with \(|N|=n\), and a finite set of posts \(A\) with \(|A|=m\). An assignment is a family \(P=(S_a)_{a\in A}\) of pairwise disjoint subsets of \(N\) whose union is \(N\); agent \(i\in S_a\) is assigned to post \(a\). Under anonymous (unweighted) congestion, the congestion (occupancy) of post \(a\) in assignment \(P\) is \(s_a=|S_a|\), and the congestion profile is \(s=(s_a)_{a\in A}\).

Each agent \(i\) has preferences over allocations \((a,s)\) consisting of a post \(a\in A\) and an integer congestion level \(s\in\{1,2,\dots,n\}\). Preferences are strictly decreasing in the congestion coordinate: for each fixed \(a\), \((a,s)\) is strictly preferred to \((a,s+1)\).

A randomized assignment induces an expected congestion profile \(\sigma=(\sigma_a)_{a\in A}\) with \(\sum_{a\in A}\sigma_a=n\) and \(\sigma_a\ge 0\). Given cardinal utilities \(u_i(a,s)\) (strictly decreasing in \(s\) for fixed \(a\)), one can extend to non-integer congestion via linear interpolation in \(s\), yielding a continuous function \(u_i(a,\sigma_a)\) for \(\sigma_a\in[1,n]\).

In the baseline model without hard bounds, a fractional expected congestion profile \(\sigma\) is called competitive if it can be generated by assigning each agent with a probability distribution supported on their competitive-demand posts, where the competitive demand of agent \(i\) at profile \(\sigma\) is
\[
D(u_i;\sigma)=\arg\max_{x\in A} u_i\bigl(x,\,\sigma_x\vee 1\;\bigr),
\]
with \(\vee\) denoting \(\max\). The paper proves existence and (qualified) uniqueness of such a competitive fractional congestion profile in the anonymous-congestion setting.

Now consider adding post-specific lower and upper bounds on congestion. Let \((\ell_a)_{a\in A}\) and \((u_a)_{a\in A}\) be integers with \(0\le \ell_a\le u_a\le n\). A deterministic assignment \(P\) is feasible with bounds if \(\ell_a\le s_a\le u_a\) for all \(a\in A\). A randomized assignment is feasible with bounds if its realized deterministic assignments satisfy these bounds (or, alternatively, if one imposes constraints on expected congestion; the paper highlights difficulty particularly due to lower bounds).

Formulate an appropriate notion of competitiveness (an envy-freeness/market-clearing-style ex post fairness concept) for congested assignment problems with anonymous congestion when each post \(a\in A\) is subject to a lower bound \(\ell_a\) (and possibly also an upper bound \(u_a\)) on its congestion. In particular, specify how competitiveness should treat posts forced to have occupancy at least \(\ell_a\) even when all agents strictly dislike being assigned there at those congestion levels, and provide a definition that can serve as a basis for selecting a fair randomized assignment in such bounded-congestion settings.

open_problem_quote:
Competitiveness is much harder to adapt. Its de
tion generalises when we
have only upper bounds, but we lose the critical \xed point argument in Lemma 1
for the existence of a randomised competitive congestion pro\le.

With lower bounds it is not clear what the de\nition of competitiveness should

subject_classification: Fair division and assignment with congestion externalities
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The paper’s question is primarily definitional/normative (what the right competitiveness notion should be under lower bounds), not a crisp universal claim that can be falsified by a finite instance. Automated search could illustrate pathologies for candidate definitions, but there is no specified target theorem to refute.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Because the task is to propose and justify an appropriate definition (and potentially prove existence/selection properties), there is no clear automatic fitness function to evaluate candidate outputs at scale without substantial human judgment about axioms and normative desiderata.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
